welcomeMessage.en: Today is undoubtedly everyone's favorite topic: Food!
welcomeMessage.zh: 毫无疑问，今天是我们每个人最喜欢的话题：美食！

welcomeMessage.en: Food is a universal language, but describing tastes, textures, and cooking methods in another language can be a bit tricky.
welcomeMessage.zh: 美食是一种通用语言，但是用另一种语言来描述味道、口感和烹饪方法可能会有些棘手。

welcomeMessage.en: We will share our favorite dishes, our worst cooking mistakes, and the strangest things we have ever eaten!
welcomeMessage.zh: 我们将分享我们最喜欢的菜肴，我们犯过的最糟糕的烹饪错误，以及我们吃过的最奇怪的东西！

questionsToThinkAbout.en: What is your absolute favorite comfort food, and why do you love it?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你绝对最喜欢的治愈系美食是什么，为什么喜欢它？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: Do you prefer cooking at home or dining out at a restaurant? Why?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你更喜欢在家做饭还是去餐厅就餐？为什么？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: Can you describe a funny or terrible cooking disaster you've experienced?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你能描述一次你经历过的有趣或糟糕的“厨房灾难”吗？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: What is the most bizarre or unusual thing you have ever tasted?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你尝过的最古怪或最不同寻常的东西是什么？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: If you had to eat only one type of cuisine for the rest of your life, what would it be?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 如果你余生只能吃一种菜系，你会选哪一种？

vocabulary.definition: A style or method of cooking, especially characteristic of a particular country or region.
vocabulary.example: "Italian cuisine is famous for its pasta and pizza."

vocabulary.definition: Food that provides nostalgic or sentimental value, often high in carbs.
vocabulary.translation: 安慰食品 / 治愈系美食
vocabulary.example: "After a long, stressful day, mac and cheese is my ultimate comfort food."
"We decided to dine out tonight instead of cooking."

vocabulary.definition: Suffering severely from hunger (often used casually).
vocabulary.example: "Let's order dinner now, I am absolutely starving!"
"Fried insects are considered a bizarre snack in some countries."

vocabulary.definition: The feel, appearance, or consistency of a substance, especially food in the mouth.
vocabulary.example: "I love the crunchy texture of fresh apples."
"This soup is a bit bland; it needs more salt and spices."

vocabulary.definition: Salty or spicy rather than sweet.
vocabulary.example: "I prefer savory snacks like potato chips over sweet candies."

vocabulary.definition: A set of instructions for preparing a particular dish.
vocabulary.example: "My grandmother gave me her secret recipe for chicken soup."

vocabulary.definition: The foods or substances combined to make a particular dish.
vocabulary.translation: 成分 / 原料 / 食材
vocabulary.example: "Do we have all the ingredients we need to bake a cake?"

meaning.en: Something that is very easy to do.

commonPhrases.example: "Don't worry about the English test tomorrow, it will be a piece of cake!"

commonPhrases.translation: 不是我的菜 / 不合我胃口

meaning.en: Not what one likes or is interested in.
meaning.zh: 不是某人喜欢或感兴趣的事物。

commonPhrases.example: "Horror movies are just not my cup of tea. I prefer comedies."
"Come on, spill the beans! What did you get Mom for her birthday?"

goals: Expand adjectives: Help the student move beyond saying food is just 'good' or 'delicious'. Encourage words like savory, tender, crispy, bland, and mouth-watering.
Storytelling: When they talk about cooking disasters, pay attention to their use of past continuous vs. past simple (e.g., 'I was boiling the water when the pot cracked.').

phases.title: Phase 1: Warm-Up Questions (10 Minutes)
phases.description: Easy food-related questions to get them talking.
questions: What did you have for breakfast today?
Are you a picky eater, or do you eat almost anything?
How often do you cook your own meals?

phases.title: Phase 2: Deep Dive (30-40 Minutes)
phases.description: More complex reflections on diet, culture, and experiences.
questions: What is your 'comfort food'? The food you immediately crave when you are feeling sad or tired.
Tell me about a time you tried a completely new cuisine. Did you like it?
Have you ever had a 'cooking disaster'? What went wrong?
In your culture, what is the most important meal of the day, and why?
If you were opening your own restaurant tomorrow, what kind of food would you serve?

phases.title: Phase 3: The 'Gross' Factor (10 Minutes)
phases.description: A fun discussion about weird foods.

sessionSummary: Today's class was incredibly fun and delicious! We focused all of our discussions around food, cooking habits, and eating out at restaurants.

We started by talking about our absolute favorite foods. Yating mentioned she has so many favorites it's hard to choose, but hot pot and sweet and sour dishes are at the top of the list. Peter shared that his favorite things are simple and healthy, like beef and milk.

Next, we debated whether it is better to cook at home or eat out. Yating made a great point that eating out is better because you don't have to clean the kitchen afterwards! Peter, on the other hand, prefers cooking at home because he finds it more convenient and comforting.

The conversation got very interesting when we talked about bizarre and strange foods. We discussed eating things like bugs and snakes, which led to a great pronunciation practice session distinguishing between the words 'snack', 'snake', and 'sneak'.

We finished the class with a fantastic and funny roleplay set in a restaurant. We practiced how to complain politely (and maybe a little angrily!) to a waiter when there is a bug in the soup or when the steak is completely cold.

keyVocabulary.example: "Hot pot is my absolute favorite food in the whole world."

keyVocabulary.translation: 芳香的 / 香气扑鼻的
keyVocabulary.example: "The soup smells so aromatic with all those fresh herbs."
"Peter thinks cooking at home is actually very convenient."
"I need to overcome my fear of trying strange and bizarre foods."
"When we make dumplings, rolling the dumpling wrappers is the hardest part."
"I am not a picky eater; I am willing to try almost anything."
"I sometimes sneak into the kitchen at midnight to eat a snack."
"Some people eat snake meat, but I am terrified of them."
"Potato chips are my absolute favorite afternoon snack."
"I am afraid of eating bugs; they look too scary!"
"Are you willing to try eating a century egg?"
"I have never eaten a snake in my entire life."
"Late at night, I always crave spicy hot pot or barbecue."
"Foreigners often think century egg is weird, but it is delicious in porridge."
"I prefer steamed dumplings over fried dumplings because they are healthier."
"If you eat spicy food every day, your stomach will get used to it."
"Excuse me, waiter! There is a bug swimming in my soup!"

grammarTips.title: Pronunciation Practice: Snack vs. Snake vs. Sneak
grammarTips.description: These three words look similar but have very different vowel sounds! Be careful not to mix them up when talking about food.
grammarTips.incorrect: "I like to eat snakes in the afternoon."
grammarTips.correct: "Snack (零食 - short 'a' sound like in 'cat'). Snake (蛇 - long 'a' sound like in 'cake'). Sneak (偷偷摸摸 - long 'e' sound like in 'week')."

grammarTips.title: Using 'Afraid of'
grammarTips.description: When you use the phrase 'afraid of', it must be followed by a noun (a thing) OR an '-ing' verb (a gerund).
grammarTips.correct: "I am afraid of bugs. (Noun) OR I am afraid of EATING bugs. (Verb-ing)"

grammarTips.title: Using 'Have never' for Life Experiences
grammarTips.description: When talking about things you have not done in your life up to this point, use 'have never' + the past participle (V3) of the verb.

grammarTips.title: At the Restaurant: How to complain politely
grammarTips.description: If there is a problem with your food, it is polite to start with 'Excuse me' before stating the problem.
grammarTips.correct: "Excuse me, could you come here? There seems to be a bug in my soup."

conversationPractice.title: Dining & Cooking Discussion Questions
conversationPractice.description: Use these questions to practice speaking with each other during your next review session:
questions: If you could only eat one country's cuisine (Chinese, Italian, Mexican, etc.) for the rest of your life, what would you choose and why?
Are you a picky eater? What is one food you absolutely refuse to eat?
Can you describe how to make your favorite dish? Do you need to fry, boil, or steam it?
What is the strangest thing you have ever eaten? Did you like it?
Roleplay: Imagine you ordered a steak medium-rare, but it arrived burnt and completely black. How would you tell the waiter?

classReview.encouragement: Amazing effort today! Your vocabulary is expanding so quickly. Keep reading these examples out loud to practice your pronunciation—especially 'snack' and 'snake'. See you next time!
